text: The surgeon initiates dissection by creating a plane beneath the inferior mesenteric vein. Subsequently, the rectal tube is mobilized from medial to lateral by making medial peritoneal cuts. The rectosigmoid and descending colon are reflected off the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric artery down to its root, clips it, and divides it beyond the origin of the left colic artery. This is followed by posterior pelvic dissection of the rectal tube.

The surgeon completes lateral peritoneal cuts over the external iliac vessels, facilitating lateral pelvic lymph node dissection. The external iliac vessels are identified and dissected, while the ureter is shifted medially. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced to the internal iliac vasculature, creating a paravascular space. Nodal tissue between the obturator internus and internal iliac vasculature is dissected and removed.

The surgeon addresses the medial nodal zone, located between the internal iliac vasculature laterally and the pelvic hypogastric medially. A space is created between these structures through gentle dissection, and the nodal tissue medial to the internal iliac vasculature is carefully dissected off the vessel branches and removed.

The surgeon clips and divides the superior vesicle pedicle, followed by the blood vessels supplying the prostate and seminal vesicles. The left ureter is clipped and divided near the bladder, then dissected off the retroperitoneum for ureter entry canastomosis. The right lateral pelvic node dissection is performed similarly, with the right ureter clipped, divided, and mobilized. The bladder is mobilized, both umbilical arteries are divided, and the bladder is taken down by entering the space of right CS.

The surgeon identifies, clips, and divides the dorsal venous complex, controlling troublesome bleeding in the area. The prostate is dissected and lifted off the pelvic floor, with packing applied to maintain a clean surgical field.

The surgeon divides the descending colon using an endostapler and clips the descending colon mesentery to the anterior abdominal wall, creating space for ureteral anastomosis. The ileocecal junction is mobilized to facilitate the placement of the ileal conduit.

The surgeon uses umbilical tape to measure the bowel length and clips to mark the proximal and distal ends of the conduit. Endo staplers are employed to divide the bowel, and bowel continuity is reestablished through a side-to-side ileo-ileostomy using endo staplers.

The surgeon spatulates the cut ends of the ureters and joins their medial walls using absorbable suture material. Digest ends are inserted into both ureters with a guide wire, and the opposite ends are inserted into the distal end of the ileal conduit. The ureteroenteric anastomosis is completed using continuous absorbable sutures.
The surgeon completes the final part of the excision via the perineal route. Subsequently, the ileal conduit is fashioned, and an end colostomy is created.
